architecture.summary: The system augments pretrained Code LLMs with constrained decoding during inference to enforce security constraints without retraining. Two techniques are proposed: (1) Constrained Beam Sampling, which adapts beam search by adding stochastic sampling and constraint-checking at each decoding step to enforce security keywords/templates while generating code tokens left-to-right; (2) adaptation of MuCoLa, a gradient-based non-autoregressive method that formulates decoding as sampling from an energy-based model using Langevin Dynamics, with a custom energy function to enforce security constraints over entire output sequences. The system operates on Code LLM probability distributions p(y|x) without modifying model weights.

biblio.title: Constrained Decoding for Secure Code Generation

claims.statement: CodeGen + Prefix-tuning achieves 71.91% SVEN security rate using Nucleus Sampling, but only 29.14% secure-pass@ using the new metric that evaluates both security and correctness, representing an overestimation of the state-of-the-art defense.
Prefix tuning sacrifices functional correctness to generate secure code, decreasing pass@ by 6.94% compared to baseline.
Constrained Beam Sampling on CodeGen achieves 51.25% secure-pass@, outperforming prefix tuning with unconstrained decoding (36.3% secure-pass@ for SVEN).
Every model with constrained decoding shows better secure-pass@ than GPT-4 with Nucleus Sampling, tested over six open-source Code LLMs of sizes ranging from 2.7B to 34B.
Different decoding methods significantly affect the security of Code LLMs; Beam Sampling has higher secure-pass@ than Nucleus Sampling for six out of seven open-source models evaluated.
Constrained decoding does not specify correctness constraints and leaves this to future work; only security constraints based on common secure coding practices are enforced.
Constrained decoding is more effective than prefix tuning to improve the security of Code LLMs, without requiring a specialized training dataset.

baseline.name: Pearce et al. security prompt dataset

claims.statement: CodeGuard+ benchmark contains 91 prompts across 34 CWEs in C/C++ and Python, with unit tests and security constraints, larger than the widely-used security prompt dataset from Pearce et al. which has 54 unique prompts covering 18 CWEs.
